sections.heading: File Storage in Android
sections.content: Android apps often need to store files such as images, videos, documents, logs, or downloaded content. Unlike structured storage systems like Room or DataStore, file storage handles raw binary or text data. Android provides several storage options depending on whether files should be private to the app, temporary, or shared with other apps. The main storage locations are internal storage, app-specific external storage, and shared media storage through MediaStore.

sections.heading: Main Storage Options
items: Internal storage — private files stored inside the app sandbox
Cache directories — temporary files the system may delete when storage is low
External app-specific storage — large files accessible through file managers
MediaStore — shared photos, videos, and audio visible to other apps
Storage Access Framework (SAF) — lets users choose files from the system picker
Assets and raw resources — read-only files bundled with the app

rows.best_for: Sensitive app data and configuration files
Large files such as exports or recordings
Photos and videos that appear in the gallery

sections.heading: Internal Storage
sections.content: Internal storage is the safest location for storing private app data. Each Android app has its own internal directory that cannot be accessed by other apps. Files are stored using context.filesDir for permanent files and context.cacheDir for temporary data. Files remain until the user clears app data or uninstalls the app.

sections.heading: External App-Specific Storage
sections.content: External app-specific directories allow apps to store larger files outside the internal sandbox. These directories are still scoped to the app but can be accessed by file manager apps. The path is obtained using getExternalFilesDir(). No storage permission is required on Android 10 and above.

sections.heading: MediaStore and Shared Media
sections.content: MediaStore is used for storing media files such as images, videos, and audio that should be visible to the user and other apps. When an app inserts media using MediaStore APIs, the file appears automatically in the device gallery. Android 10 introduced Scoped Storage, which requires apps to use MediaStore instead of direct file paths for shared media.

sections.heading: Best Practices
items: Perform file operations on background threads using Dispatchers.IO
Use internal storage for private or sensitive data
Use cache directories for temporary files
Use MediaStore for photos and videos that should appear in the gallery
Use FileProvider when sharing files with other apps

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Android provides several storage mechanisms depending on file visibility and size. Internal storage is best for private app data, cache directories for temporary files, external app-specific directories for larger files, and MediaStore for shared media. Modern Android versions enforce scoped storage, encouraging developers to use MediaStore and the Storage Access Framework instead of direct file system access.
